title: MiFID II Best Execution — Client Limit Order Price Improvement vs Top of Book

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'unspecified' → 'Art.27'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

dataSources: `index=trading` `sourcetype="ems:fill"` (order_type, limit_px, best_bid, best_ask, exec_px, side, client_segment)
app: EMS and market data via HEC
spl:
index=trading sourcetype="ems:fill" order_type="limit" client_segment="retail" earliest=-30d
| eval improvement_bps=if(side="B",(best_ask-exec_px)/best_ask*10000,(exec_px-best_bid)/best_bid*10000)
| where improvement_bps < 0
| stats count as worse_than_tob, median(improvement_bps) as med_imp by venue
| sort - worse_than_tob
description: Limit orders should receive fair treatment relative to displayed liquidity. Measuring frequency and magnitude of price improvement versus top-of-book at execution time supports conduct narratives for retail protection.
value: Limit orders should receive fair treatment relative to displayed liquidity. Measuring frequency and magnitude of price improvement versus top-of-book at execution time supports conduct narratives for retail protection.
grandmaExplanation: Limit orders should receive fair treatment relative to displayed liquidity — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Validate `best_bid/ask` timestamps align with fill; (2) handle inverted books; (3) retail-only scope; (4) investigation workflow; (5) link to smart order router version.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: EMS and market data via HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=trading` `sourcetype="ems:fill"` (order_type, limit_px, best_bid, best_ask, exec_px, side, client_segment).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Validate `best_bid/ask` timestamps align with fill; (2) handle inverted books; (3) retail-only scope; (4) investigation workflow; (5) link to smart order router version.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trading sourcetype="ems:fill" order_type="limit" client_segment="retail" earliest=-30d
| eval improvement_bps=if(side="B",(best_ask-exec_px)/best_ask*10000,(exec_px-best_bid)/best_bid*10000)
| where improvement_bps < 0
| stats count as worse_than_tob, median(improvement_bps) as med_imp by venue
| sort - worse_than_tob
```

Understanding this SPL

**MiFID II Best Execution — Client Limit Order Price Improvement vs Top of Book** — Limit orders should receive fair treatment relative to displayed liquidity. Measuring frequency and magnitude of price improvement versus top-of-book at execution time supports conduct narratives for retail protection.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=trading` `sourcetype="ems:fill"` (order_type, limit_px, best_bid, best_ask, exec_px, side, client_segment). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): EMS and market data via HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trading; **sourcetype**: ems:fill. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trading, sourcetype="ems:fill", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **improvement_bps** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where improvement_bps < 0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by venue** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Histogram (improvement_bps), Table (venues), Single value (worse fills).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
